text: Media theorist Douglas Rushkoff coined the phrase 'present shock' to describe a culture so saturated with real-time information streams that narrative — the ability to connect past and future into coherent story — begins to dissolve. Where television gave us a story arc, the feed gives us an eternal present of disconnected updates, each one crowding out the last. Rushkoff argues this is not merely uncomfortable; it impairs agency. To act intentionally requires imagining consequences that unfold over time. A mind trapped in the perpetual now, perpetually notified, becomes reactive rather than deliberate — responding to stimuli rather than pursuing goals.
question: According to Rushkoff, why does 'present shock' impair human agency?

options.text: Real-time feeds expose users to more stressful news than they can process
Acting intentionally requires imagining consequences over time, which present shock prevents
Constant notifications physically exhaust the prefrontal cortex
Narrative storytelling is the only medium that builds empathy
